content:
Wild berries are the best whole food antioxidant

Blackberries are a low-carbohydrate fruit that is nutritional powerhouse. Its deep purple hue increases their antioxidant power. Blackberries are an excellent source of fiber and vitamin C, and contain a full day's allowance of manganese.

Strawberries are packed with vitamins, fiber, and particularly high levels of antioxidants known as polyphenols. They are among the top 20 fruits in antioxidant capacity and are a good source of manganese and potassium.

Red Raspberries contain strong antioxidants such as vitamin C, quercetin and gallic acid that fight against cancer, heart and circulatory disease and age-related decline. They are high in ellagic acid, a known chemo-preventative, and have been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties.

Black raspberries are rich in vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin E and folic acid, minerals such as calcium, selenium, zinc as well as fibre, anthocyanins, quercetin, phytosterols, chlorogenic acid, phenolics and bioactive compounds.

Boysenberries are good source of fiber, vitamin C and potassium. They also contain a host of other essential vitamins and minerals like folate, vitamin E, vitamin B and manganese.

Cherries are a potent source of antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds. Cherries slow down aging and ward off chronic illnesses including heart disease, cancer, alzheimer's, diabetes, and obesity. Cherries also contain fiber, vitamin C, carotenoids, and anthocyanins, each of which may help play a role in cancer prevention.

Blueberries contain a plant compound called anthocyanin. This gives blueberries both their blue color and many of their health benefits. Blueberries can help heart health, bone strength, skin health, blood pressure, diabetes management, cancer prevention, and mental health.

Cranberries has very high nutrient and antioxidant content. The nutrients in cranberries have been linked with prevention of age-related oxidative damage, reduction in heart and other chronic diseases, and prevention of urinary tract (UTI) and other infections.

Mulberries provide unusually high levels of protein and iron for a fruit, and are also a rich source of vitamin C, fiber, calcium, and antioxidants.

Why do we need antioxidant?

If you face electronic devices like computers or smartphones daily, there is a good chance you are bombarded by free radicals daily. Daily exposure to free radicals causes you to feel tired from work and it may also affect your sleep quality. Ever wonder why you always fall sick?

Free radicals cause oxidative stress on the body which can damage DNA, leading to cancer; compromise cell walls, allowing substances into the cell that aren't supposed to be there; and make LDL-cholesterol stick to blood vessels, causing atherosclerosis.

ANTIOXIDANT

Antioxidants are nutrients that help stop or limit damage caused by free radicals. Your body uses antioxidants to balance free radicals. This keeps them from causing damage to other cells. Antioxidants can protect and reverse some of the damage. They also boost your immunity.

Antioxidants are therefore vital for our health as they are implicated in healthy ageing, reducing the virulence of chronic disease, improving cognition and mental health. Ultimately they work to maintain and improve general health and wellbeing.

Antioxidant is measured by a clinical test called ORAC (Oxidative Reactive Absorbance Capacity) developed by USDA. Humans need about 4000 TE daily for good health.

Benefits of antioxidant:
• Boosts a stronger immune system and increases resistance to viruses & infections
• Improves blood circulation to maintain a healthy circulatory system and prevents clots that can lead to heart attacks and strokes
• Prevents free radicals from damaging cells and causing symptoms of premature aging
• Helps protect skin from harmful ultraviolet radiation
• Reduces oxidation damage that can cause cancer
• Protect eye tissues from inflammation, cataracts, glaucoma & macular degeneration

Who needs antioxidant?
• People who fall sick easily due to infections by bacteria or viruses
• People who face the computer screen or electronic devices daily
• Smokers or people who are exposed to cigarette smoke
• People who consume large amounts of fatty food
• People who are exposed to excessive sunlight
• People who are exposed to air pollution
• People who consume alcohol
• People who are exposed to chemicals and toxins
• People who have high blood sugar levels
• People to do intense and prolonged exercise, which causes tissue damage

What are polyphenols?

Polyphenols are a group of natural coloring pigments in some plants. Research indicates that polyphenols have antioxidant characteristics with potential health benefits. These polyphenol antioxidants may reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease and cancer.

FLAVONOLS: Resveratrol, Quercetin, Luteolin, Apigenin, Kaempferol, Myricetin and Isorhamnetin
Clinical Evidence: Neuroprotective, strengthens cardiovascular system and anti cancer

FLAVONES: Luteolin and Apigenin
Clinical Evidence: Anti allergy, anti inflammatory

FLAVAN-3-OLS: Catechin, Gallocatechin, Epicatechin, Epigallocatechin 3-gallate, Theaflavin, Theaflavin 3-gallate, Theaflavin 3,3'-digallate, Thearubigins. Strengthening of immune system

ANTHOCYANIDINS: Cyanidin, Delphinidin, Malvidin, Pelargonidin, Peonidin
Clinical Evidence: Anti tumor and anti cancer

Ellagic Acid: Polyphenol found in raspberries, strawberries and cranberries
Clinical Evidence: Anti carcinogen and anti mutagen
- Slows growth of abnormal colon cells
- Promotes wound healing
- Reduces occurrence of birth defect
- Prevents the development of cells infected with human papilloma virus (HPV) which is linked to cervical cancer
- Reduces and reverse chemically induced liver fibrosis
- Promotes apoptotic growth (natural death) of prostate cancer cells. The apoptotic process triggered by this antioxidant ellagitannin has beneficial effects on breast, lung, esophageal and skin cancer (melanoma)
- Inhibition of carcinogenesis by ellagic acid has been demonstrated in animals with esophagus, tongue, lung, colon, liver and skin tumors

USDA database for polyphenol (flavonoid) content in different species of berries (2003)
Blackberries: Flavonols ✓, Flavan-3-OLS ✓
Blueberries: Flavonols ✓, Flavan-3-OLS ✓, Anthocyanidins ✓
Cherries: Flavonols ✓, Flavones ✓, Flavan-3-OLS ✓, Anthocyanidins ✓
Cranberries: Flavonols ✓, Flavan-3-OLS ✓, Ellagic Acid ✓
Raspberries: Flavonols ✓, Flavan-3-OLS ✓, Anthocyanidins ✓, Ellagic Acid ✓
Strawberries: Flavonols ✓, Flavones ✓, Flavan-3-OLS ✓, Ellagic Acid ✓
Boysenberries: Flavonols ✓, Anthocyanidins ✓
